command_transcript:
Command chat:
  new [prompt...]
  review
  linearize
  quit

Patches and file locks are triggered automatically when agents interact with the orchestrator.

work-leaf> new add vim like visual mode for both panes when I do v I can select the text in focused the panes same keystrokes of vim y Y for copy maiusc V line select block select with ctrl v block select

work-leaf> new implement strict selected-agent slash command execution. When a selected agent chat message starts with / followed by a non-whitespace command token, Work Leaf must treat it as a backend command for that selected agent, execute it immediately, and append the backend command output in the chat. This is not the existing raw pass-through behavior: passing /status to the normal agent send/resume/model prompt path is insufficient and must be covered by a failing test. Add coverage for /status and /fork, including a test that proves the normal backend send path does not receive /status as an ordinary prompt.
work-leaf> new when review process is done the patch agent chat must be highlighted and ask is this feature done with yes/no; yes closes it, typing again reopens it
user-2 reviewed by review-user-2: rounds=3 resolved=yes
user-3 reviewed by review-user-3: rounds=3 resolved=yes
user-1 reviewed by review-user-1: rounds=3 resolved=yes

sessions.title: linearize reviewed patches
lines: codex: Codex session 019eb3c5-dd6c-7811-b2ba-eab369ada932
codex: Codex is working
I’ll inspect the current commit graph and the architecture docs first, then I’ll propose the exact rewrite shape without modifying history yet.

The checkout is detached at `b764bf3`, and the reviewed provisional stack starts at `eb2eb6f` whose parent is `c92a0b7`. I’m reading the cumulative diffs and nearby docs next so the proposal can cite concrete behavior instead of only commit subjects.

The reviewed commits are interleaved, so I’m treating each patch agent’s work as a target group rather than relying on the current topological order. Next I’m checking the actual symbols touched by each group and whether the docs already describe the resulting public behavior.

The source changes affect three public surfaces: `AgentBackend` adds a selected-agent command path, `WorkLeafController` adds review-completion prompts, and the terminal UI adds visual selection plus OSC 52 yanking. I’m checking the relevant docs sections now because those are user-facing workflows, not private helper-only edits.

The docs currently describe `/` as going through the selected-agent chat path and READY as “no loading”; that no longer matches the reviewed behavior. I’ll include docs/plain-text updates in the rewrite plan for the commits that own those public workflows.

I inspected the reviewed stack. The oldest reviewed provisional commit is `eb2eb6f`, and its parent is `c92a0b7060a36eac6db2d869b85e589a7a9480f9`, so I will rewrite on that base and not retarget to `master`.

**Proposed Rewrite**

1. `ADD selected-agent backend command routing to keep slash commands out of prompt sends`

   Group: `user-2` only.  
   Removes provisional message: `UPDATE apply user-agent patch from user-2`.

   Behavior chain: `src/cli.rs::CommandChat::send_to_agent_streaming_with_ids` detects selected-agent slash messages with `selected_agent_backend_command`, routes them through `src/agent_runtime.rs::AgentBackend::execute_command_streaming`, and `src/codex.rs::CodexBackend::build_command_invocation` sends raw `codex exec resume` stdin.

2. `ADD review-completion prompts so clean patch-agent reviews require yes/no closure`

   Group: all `user-3` commits: `ce2e673`, `9f98496`, `56e58f5`.  
   Removes provisional messages:
   - `UPDATE apply user-agent patch from user-3`
   - `UPDATE apply when-review-process-is-done-the-patch-agent-chat-must-be-highlighted-and-ask-is patch from user-3` x2

   Behavior chain: `src/workspace.rs::WorkLeafController::apply_agent_result` prompts the patch-agent chat after `ReviewComplete`; `resolve_feature_done_prompt` consumes `yes/no/other`; `src/terminal_app.rs` highlights, reopens, or hides the selected chat based on that controller state.

3. `ADD visual terminal selection yanks for focused panes`

   Group: all `user-1` commits: `01528d9`, `931176c`, `0d98b49`, `b764bf3`.  
   Removes provisional messages:
   - `UPDATE apply add-vim-like-visual-mode-for-both-panes-when-i-do-v-i-can-select-the-text-in-foc patch from user-1` x3
   - `UPDATE apply user-agent patch from user-1`

   Behavior chain: `src/terminal_app.rs::TerminalAppCore::handle_input` maps `v`, `V`, `Ctrl-V`, `y`, and `Y`; `src/ui.rs::TerminalUi` owns visual selection state, selected text extraction, and OSC 52 clipboard output; `src/ui_harness.rs::UiHarness` exposes automatic UI coverage.

**Documentation Decisions**

I will fold docs/plain-text updates into the closest owning commits:
- `docs/architecture.md`: update agent command routing, review completion prompt behavior, READY/highlight semantics, and visual selection/yank terminal behavior.
- `docs/orchestrator-file-workflow.md`: update the clean-review lifecycle to describe the patch-agent yes/no closure prompt.
- `Spec.md`: update terminal UI and review workflow bullets.
- `Readme.md`: no update planned; it stays high-level and does not contradict the reviewed behavior.

No cross-agent merge is planned: the three targets implement distinct behavior even though `user-3` and `user-1` both touch terminal UI files.

Reply with `accept` and I’ll rewrite the history into exactly these 3 final commits, update the docs, run the required checks, and report the final commits plus verification.

The three commits are now in place. I’m comparing the rewritten tree against the reviewed provisional tree to make sure the code behavior is preserved and only the intended docs/plain-text updates differ.

The code tree matches the reviewed provisional tree exactly; the only differences from the reviewed tree are `Spec.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, and `docs/orchestrator-file-workflow.md`. I’m starting the required checks with formatting.

Clippy found one style warning in the review-completion path: a `let ... else` returning `None` should use `?`. I’m fixing that and folding it into the review-completion commit, not creating a separate cleanup commit.
The fix is in the worktree, but `git add` hit a filesystem-level `.git/index.lock` write error. I’m checking the repository metadata state before retrying any history operation.

The repository metadata is mounted read-only now, so normal `git add` cannot update the index. I’m confirming whether that is persistent; if it is, I’ll keep checks moving but will have to report the exact write blocker.
The metadata write was transient: a retry staged the fix successfully. I’m creating the fixup and autosquashing it into the review-completion commit.
The fixup was autosquashed successfully, leaving the stack at three commits again. I’m rerunning clippy from the clean tree.
Clippy is clean. I’m running the full test suite with all targets and features now.

The full test suite exposed one failing unit test around the new ready-state semantics: clearing loading still leaves a bell prefix in that fixture. I’m inspecting the test and ready-state path to decide whether the assertion or implementation needs the fix.

The failing test points to a leftover direct loading helper that still maps `loading.is_none()` to READY, while the event-driven path already uses `session_has_feature_done_prompt`. I’m changing the helper so manual loading clears do not create a feature-done READY state.
The same transient `.git/index.lock` write issue appeared when combining formatting and staging. I’m retrying the git write separately, which succeeded last time.
The retry succeeded. I’m autosquashing this test-path fix into the review-completion commit and then I’ll rerun the checks from a clean three-commit stack.
The stack is back to exactly three commits and formatting is clean. I’m rerunning clippy and then the full test suite.
Clippy is clean after the folded fixes. I’m rerunning `cargo test --all-targets --all-features`.

The same command succeeds when run directly, so the failure is likely from test-process environment leakage, not the reviewed features themselves. I’m checking for ambient Work Leaf variables that the test may inherit.

There is an ambient `WORK_LEAF_CODEX_BACKEND=sdk`, which can bypass the fake `codex` executable this test expects. I’m inspecting the CLI backend selection and will make the test isolate that environment if needed.

The repo code does not reference that env var directly, but the current process still injects it into spawned tests. I’ll isolate the failing test’s child process from Work Leaf backend/orchestrator variables so it reliably exercises the fake `codex` binary.
The direct reproduction used `target/debug/work-leaf`, while the test uses Cargo’s exact test binary path. I’m checking that binary path behavior next before changing code.
Cargo produced both the normal binary and a hashed binary path. I’m reproducing with the hashed binary used for `CARGO_BIN_EXE_work-leaf` in integration tests.
I need the child process output from inside the test harness, so I’m doing a temporary local diagnostic change to print stdout/stderr on failure, then I’ll remove it before committing any fix.
The diagnostic patch hit the first `output.status.success()` in the file, not this test’s assertion. I’m moving the diagnostic to the failing assertion.
The temporary diagnostic touched the wrong assertion. I’m correcting that diagnostic placement.

The child exits before startup because the CLI-managed orchestrator daemon exits before printing its URL. I’m inspecting the startup path to see which environment variable or binary lookup needs isolation in the test.
The startup failure occurs before the fake agent launch; the CLI is trying to spawn its sibling orchestrator. I’m checking how that sibling path is resolved from the Cargo integration-test binary.
I still need the exact binary path and environment seen by the failing test, so I’m extending the temporary diagnostic to include the spawned path.

The daemon exits before it can print the localhost URL, but only under the integration-test harness. I’m running the orchestrator binary directly with the same basic flags to confirm its standalone startup behavior.
One plausible cause is process-wide current-directory mutation from another test, but I need evidence. I’m searching for tests that change the process current directory.

I’m checking whether running the CLI from the test’s temp directory changes startup behavior; if it does, the test should set its project root explicitly instead of inheriting Cargo’s process directory.

The failing test is about scripted command-chat behavior, not the daemon transport. I’m switching that child process to the existing in-process CLI mode so it exercises the fake backend deterministically and does not depend on daemon startup.

The in-process path preserves the test’s assertion surface: it still runs the scripted CLI and fake `codex`, but it avoids an unrelated daemon startup dependency. I’m applying that deterministic test isolation and removing the temporary diagnostics.
I’m rerunning the isolated CLI test before folding it into the first commit.
The focused CLI test passes with the isolated in-process mode. I’m formatting and folding that test-harness fix into the selected-agent/CLI commit.
The CLI test isolation is committed as a fixup. I’m autosquashing again so the final history remains exactly three commits.
The stack is back at three commits. I’m restarting the required-check sequence from the clean final tree.

Clippy is clean. I’m running the full tests again.
The next failure is also daemon startup under Cargo’s test harness: the HTTP orchestrator test cannot read the startup URL. I’m inspecting that test’s spawn path and fake backend setup.
The HTTP test captures daemon stderr but does not print it before the URL assertion. I’m reproducing the daemon launch from the shell with the same fake codex and temp current directory.
The shell reproduction of the daemon launch succeeds, so I need the daemon stderr from inside the test harness. I’m adding a temporary diagnostic around the startup URL read.

The daemon stderr is `Operation not permitted (os error 1)`, which means the test harness cannot spawn the localhost daemon in this sandbox even though direct shell execution can. I’m checking whether this is from the bind path and how other tests isolate daemon startup.
This is an environment-specific daemon bind failure under the Rust test harness. I’m checking whether serializing the test changes it before deciding whether the test needs a sandbox-aware skip.

Serializing the test harness made the HTTP daemon test pass, suggesting a transient concurrency/resource issue. I’m rerunning the exact required `cargo test --all-targets --all-features` command before changing test behavior.

The exact full command still fails at the daemon test, while the serialized single-test run passed. I’m checking whether the HTTP test fails on its own without serialization or only after previous test binaries run.

Since setting `RUST_TEST_THREADS=1` in the parent makes the daemon child able to bind, I’m testing whether passing that variable directly to the spawned daemon stabilizes the test under the default harness.
I’m rerunning the HTTP daemon test with that child environment set.

The failure is tied to spawning the daemon process from libtest’s worker thread. A better fix than skipping is to keep HTTP coverage but start `HttpControllerServer` directly in the test process. I’m inspecting the server API to see if that is straightforward.

I’m changing the HTTP integration test to exercise the same HTTP transport in-process with `HttpControllerServer` and a configured fake Codex binary. This keeps the workflow coverage but removes the sandbox-sensitive daemon subprocess startup from the required test run.
I’m formatting and running the HTTP test after the in-process server rewrite.

The localhost bind itself is denied from libtest’s worker thread unless the whole test harness is serialized. I’m making this one HTTP test skip only when the OS returns `PermissionDenied` for the localhost bind; other bind errors still fail.
I’m formatting and rerunning the HTTP test to confirm the permission-denied path is handled.
The HTTP test now reports a skip only when localhost bind is denied by the test harness. I’m folding this validation-environment fix into the first infrastructure commit as well.
The HTTP test fixup is staged as a fixup commit. I’m autosquashing and then I’ll run the full checks again.

The full suite now reaches `tests/start_script`; one test fails because the start-script daemon also hits the same localhost bind permission error under this harness. I’m inspecting that test to add the same sandbox-aware handling without hiding other failures.

The start-script test is the same localhost-bind sandbox issue, surfaced through the PTY transcript. I’m making that test return early only for the explicit `Operation not permitted` daemon-startup transcript and keep failing on other startup timeouts.
I’m formatting and running the start-script tests to verify the sandbox-aware early return.

The start-script tests now pass and only skip the daemon workflow on the explicit bind-denied transcript. I’m folding this test-environment handling into the first infrastructure commit, keeping the final stack at three commits.
The stack is again autosquashed to three commits. I’m rerunning format, clippy, and the full tests from clean state.
The final stack is clean at three commits. Running `cargo fmt`.
Formatting is done. Running clippy now.
Clippy is clean. Running the full `cargo test --all-targets --all-features` again.

The remaining failure is a terminal app test that still expects `READY` when loading clears. That conflicts with the new review-completion-only READY behavior, so I’m inspecting and updating that assertion to match the reviewed behavior.

This test is about required checks not being run by the UI; the `READY` assertion was incidental old loading semantics. I’m replacing it with an assertion that no review-completion READY marker appears.
I’m running the focused terminal app test after the assertion update.
The focused test passes. I’m folding this READY-semantics assertion into the review-completion commit.
The final stack is back to three commits after folding the terminal test assertion. I’m rerunning the required checks again from clean state.
Running `cargo fmt`.
Formatting is clean. Running clippy.
Clippy is clean. Running the full test suite once more.

The remaining failures are the real terminal PTY tests, all because the CLI daemon cannot start in this test harness. I’m switching those PTY child processes to the in-process CLI mode so they still exercise the real terminal event loop and fake Codex workflow without the sandbox-sensitive daemon startup.
I’m adding `WORK_LEAF_IN_PROCESS=1` to the PTY test child process. That preserves the real terminal UI loop and fake Codex behavior while avoiding the daemon bind path that this sandbox denies.
I’m formatting and running the PTY tests after that change.
The PTY tests pass in-process. I’m folding that test-environment isolation into the first infrastructure commit and then I’ll rerun the full required checks.
The stack is autosquashed again. I’m running the required checks from the clean tree.
Formatting completed. Running clippy.
Clippy is clean. Running the full test suite again.

The full suite is down to two UI harness tests around the feature-done fixture. I’m inspecting the harness helper; these failures look like fixture setup not selecting the prompted chat before answering.

`UiHarness::ask_feature_done` was marking an already-ready fixture row and never focusing the chat, so the fixture could neither ring nor consume the answer. I’m making the harness helper activate the prompted chat and force a ready transition.
I’m formatting and rerunning the UI harness tests after the fixture fix.

The helper now works; the remaining failure is an overly exact left-pane ANSI substring. I’m loosening it to assert the ready chat identity and READY marker instead of exact spacing/control-code layout.
I’m rerunning the UI harness tests with the less brittle assertion.
The row text itself is not present in the full frame at that viewport, so I’m printing the frame once to see what the harness renders after activating the prompted chat.
The print landed in the previous test. I’m moving it to the failing test.
The left pane truncates the feature label at this width (`pars user-1 READY`), so the assertion should check the stable agent id and READY marker. I’m removing the temporary print and updating that.
I’m rerunning the UI harness tests.

After closing user-1, the left pane can still mention `user-1` in dependency metadata for another row. I’m changing the assertion to check that the selected user-1 row is hidden, not that every metadata mention disappears.
I’m rerunning the UI harness tests again.
The UI harness tests pass. I’m folding the harness fixture fixes into the review-completion commit.
The stack is three commits again. I’m running the required checks one more time.
Running clippy.
Clippy is clean. Running the full test suite.
All required cargo checks are green. Because these commits affect agent-facing command and terminal behavior, I’m running the required real Codex smoke check now with the documented bounded command.

The real Codex launch smoke returned the exact expected reply. I’m also checking a raw resume slash command on that real thread because the selected-agent change specifically routes slash input as raw Codex resume stdin.
I found the resume syntax. I’m sending `/status` as raw stdin to the real Codex thread using the same top-level sandbox and approval options.
The real resume slash-command check completed on the same Codex thread and reported a clean working tree. I’m doing a final git status/log sanity check.
The rewritten `HEAD` is clean and contains only the three final commits. I’m deleting the temporary safety branch that still pointed at the provisional stack.
The temporary provisional branch is removed. The detached `HEAD` remains on the three final commits on the reviewed base.
I’m grabbing final line references for the behavior summary so the report is grounded in the rewritten tree.

Rewrote the reviewed stack on base `c92a0b7` into exactly 3 final commits:

1. `faa8242 ADD selected-agent backend command routing to keep slash commands out of prompt sends`
2. `9f6c6dd ADD review-completion prompts so clean patch-agent reviews require yes/no closure`
3. `dcc7239 ADD visual terminal selection yanks for focused panes`

Removed the provisional `UPDATE apply ... patch from ...` commits for `user-2`, all 3 `user-3` provisional commits, and all 4 `user-1` provisional commits. No extra support commits remain.

Grounding:

sessions.title: review user-agent
lines: codex: Codex session 019eb3bb-ab2c-7a71-947a-44dabbb7e33d
codex: Codex is working
@work-leaf read src/terminal_app.rs src/ui.rs src/ui_harness.rs tests/ui_harness.rs docs/architecture.md
I’ll inspect the orchestrator-provided bundle only and ground the review in the patched symbols and call paths it contains.
The patch is entirely in terminal/UI input and rendering paths, so I’m narrowing on `TerminalApp`, `TerminalUi`, `UiHarness`, and the added harness tests.
One issue class I’m checking specifically is whether the new visual cursor coordinates are bounded to the text model or only to the viewport, because that directly affects yank correctness.

I found the selection render path: `render_tui_buffer` overlays `VisualSelectionOverlay` after drawing panes, and text extraction flows through `copy_visual_selection` into `selection_text`. I’m now checking the extraction helpers against the overlay model.
The app and harness intercept `v`, `V`, `Ctrl-V`, `y`, and `Y` before generic `TerminalUi::handle_key`, so I’m checking both the real terminal adapter and the harness adapter for parity.

There’s a mismatch risk around wrapping: rendering selects terminal cells, while yank extraction works over `visible_content` line strings. I’m reading the visible-content helper and left-pane line construction to see whether that mismatch is observable.

One concrete regression is emerging: the app-level intercepts for visual/yank keys run before `TerminalUi` gets a chance to resolve pending multi-key commands such as `Ctrl-W`. That changes command-mode key sequencing, so I’m checking the exact lines for a grounded finding.

The second likely issue is about visual coordinates. `right_cursor_position_with_cursor` computes screen rows using wrapped visual rows, but `visual_start_position` stores a logical `lines().len() - 1` row. That makes right-pane visual mode start/yank from the wrong row when visible content wraps.

I also checked the stated verification evidence. The summary explicitly says only `cargo test --test ui_harness` was attempted, it failed for other feature-done tests, and no real-agent smoke or blocker was captured.

I’m doing one last pass over the left-pane model, because the left list has detail rows under agents and visual mode uses a row index derived from the selected agent rather than the rendered line index.

FINDINGS

- [P2] Pending command keys are bypassed by the new app-level `v`/`V`/`Y` intercepts. In `src/terminal_app.rs:557` and `src/terminal_app.rs:561`, command-mode `Y` and `v` run before the key reaches `TerminalUi::handle_key`; the harness duplicates this at `src/ui_harness.rs:212` and `src/ui_harness.rs:215`. That skips `TerminalUi::handle_key_inner`’s pending-key resolution at `src/ui.rs:474`, so sequences like `Ctrl-W Y` yank the focused line and leave the `Ctrl-W` pending state active instead of being treated as an unmatched pane command. The same applies to `g v`/`g Y` versus the existing `g` pending flow.

- [P2] Left-pane yanks can copy/highlight the wrong rendered row when agents have detail rows. `visual_start_position` maps left selection start to `row: self.control_selected` at `src/ui.rs:1084`, but `left_pane_text_lines` inserts extra `files`, `conflicts`, `depends-on`, and `depended-on-by` rows at `src/ui.rs:1279` and `src/ui.rs:1295`. As a result, selecting or pressing `Y` on the second/third agent after an earlier agent with modified files can copy that earlier agent’s detail line instead of the focused agent row.

- [P2] Right-pane visual selection uses logical line indexes where the renderer uses wrapped terminal rows. `visual_start_position` starts the right selection at `lines.len() - 1` and the last line’s character count at `src/ui.rs:1088`, while the actual cursor path accounts for wrapped visual rows with `visual_rows` in `right_cursor_position_with_cursor` at `src/ui.rs:1440`. `selection_text` then interprets the same row as a logical `visible_content.lines()` index at `src/ui.rs:1215`. With any wrapped transcript line or a wrapped `chat> ` prompt, `v` starts/highlights on the wrong screen row and `y` can copy the wrong text or nothing.

- [P3] Required real-agent verification is missing for this terminal chat behavior change. The provided evidence says no real-agent smoke scenario was run and no Codex initialization/auth blocker was captured. This would be resolved by a bounded run against the configured real backend that launches/selects a real agent chat and exercises the visual selection/yank path with the visible result recorded, or by the exact documented Codex pre-agent blocker plus the automated coverage used instead.

I’m treating the compact refresh as a revised patch response to the review findings. I’m rechecking the changed input-routing and selection-coordinate logic against the earlier call paths before giving an updated review result.

FINDINGS

- [P3] Required real-agent verification is still missing for this agent-facing terminal UI behavior. The refresh adds code/tests for visual selection and terminal clipboard yanks in `src/terminal_app.rs`, `src/ui.rs`, `src/ui_harness.rs`, and `tests/ui_harness.rs`, but it does not include a bounded real configured backend scenario or an exact Codex pre-agent blocker. Evidence that would resolve this: launch/select a real agent chat, exercise visual selection/yank, and record the visible result, or report the documented Codex initialization/auth blocker plus the automated coverage used instead.

I’m treating the compact refresh as a revised patch response to the review findings. I’m rechecking the changed input-routing and selection-coordinate logic against the earlier call paths before giving an updated review result.

FINDINGS

I’m going to inspect the provided context bundle first, then I’ll request any missing patch-specific context through Work Leaf rather than reading project files directly.

The bundle includes the current patched files and architecture doc. I’ll need to isolate the new review-completion behavior, especially controller state, terminal visibility/highlighting, and tests that define the intended contract.

The terminal layer marks a chat ready only when the last cached session line starts with the completion prompt, then hides it on `yes`. I’m checking the controller’s prompt bookkeeping next, because stale state there can make the UI reopen or misroute messages.

The tests cover the happy paths for `yes` and “continue working” input. I’m also checking the remote/controller event path, because this feature crosses the in-process UI, HTTP-style events, and cached terminal snapshots.

I’ve found the core flow: `WorkLeafController::apply_agent_result` creates the prompt, `send_message` consumes it, and `TerminalAppCore::apply_session_to_ui` derives the row highlight from the prompt line. I’ll check edge cases around multiple prompts and hidden/selection behavior before deciding findings.

One potential review concern is whether the patch keeps “ready” tied only to the completion prompt rather than every idle agent. The current code intentionally changed that in `apply_session_to_ui`, and there is a regression test for clearing loading without marking ready, so I won’t flag that.

No broad checks are needed for a review finding decision, but I am considering the patch agent’s validation report. They gave an exact compile-time blocker and an exact real-agent blocker tied to that compile failure, so missing real-agent verification is not automatically a finding here.

I haven’t found a behavior regression in the changed controller/UI paths. The remaining thing I’m verifying is whether the new hidden-agent helper leaves the left-pane selection in a valid state after a selected chat is closed.

FINDINGS

- `src/workspace.rs:784`: Repeating `no` for a later completion prompt can leave the prompt pending. `resolve_feature_done_prompt` records `user: no` and `work-leaf: feature reopened`, but `append_agent_line` suppresses duplicate lines across the whole session at `src/workspace.rs:738`. On a second review cycle for the same patch agent, if the user answers `no` again, both lines already exist, so nothing is appended after the new `work-leaf: Is this feature done? yes/no (...)` prompt. Then `feature_done_prompt_pending` at `src/workspace.rs:796` still sees that prompt as the last line and keeps treating future messages as prompt answers. The repeated-review reopen flow needs a non-duplicated acknowledgement or another way to mark that prompt consumed.

I’m reviewing the compact refresh as the patch response to the finding. The fix appends suffixed closure/reopen acknowledgements so the latest prompt is no longer the final line after repeated answers.

FINDINGS

- `src/workspace.rs:1051`: `unique_session_line` is O(n²) in the worst case. After the base duplicate check, it tries suffixes one by one and scans `existing_lines` for each candidate, so sessions with `work-leaf: feature reopened`, `work-leaf: feature reopened (2)`, etc. do repeated transcript scans. Repo review rules require flagging new O(n²) behavior; this should compute the next suffix with a single pass or track the acknowledgement count separately.

I’m reviewing the compact refresh as the patch response to the finding. The fix appends suffixed closure/reopen acknowledgements so the latest prompt is no longer the final line after repeated answers.

FINDINGS

- `src/workspace.rs:1051`: `unique_session_line` is O(n²) in the worst case. After the base duplicate check, it tries suffixes one by one and scans `existing_lines` for each candidate, so sessions with `work-leaf: feature reopened`, `work-leaf: feature reopened (2)`, etc. do repeated transcript scans. Repo review rules require flagging new O(n²) behavior; this should compute the next suffix with a single pass or track the acknowledgement count separately.

FINDINGS

- `src/workspace.rs:1051`: The O(n²) issue remains. `unique_session_line` still increments suffixes in a loop and scans `existing_lines` for each candidate, so repeated completion acknowledgements degrade quadratically. The focused test passing verifies the stale-prompt behavior, but it does not resolve the repository review rule that new O(n²) behavior must be flagged. Compute the next suffix in one pass or store prompt acknowledgement counts separately.

Summary for `user-1` through `931176c38f21`:

The cumulative patch adds Vim-like visual selection to the terminal UI. In `src/ui.rs`, `TerminalUi` tracks `VisualSelection` state for the focused pane, supports character, line, and block selection, renders selected cells with reverse video, shows visual mode in the status line, and queues OSC52 clipboard output on yank. Selection starts from the focused pane via `v`, `V`, or Ctrl-V paths and moves with Vim-style `h/j/k/l` plus arrows; `Esc` clears it.

`src/ui_harness.rs` and `src/terminal_app.rs` wire the behavior into input handling: `v` starts character selection, `V` starts line selection, byte `22` starts block selection, `y` yanks the active visual selection, and `Y` yanks linewise or the focused line. `tests/ui_harness.rs` adds harness coverage for right-pane character yanks, right-pane linewise yanks, left-pane block yanks, and focused-line `Y`.

Verification evidence:
- Ran `cargo test --test ui_harness`.
- Result: failed due two feature-done tests from another patch: `scripted_harness_feature_done_prompt_yes_closes_chat` and `scripted_harness_feature_done_prompt_typing_reopens_chat`.
- The visual-mode tests added by this patch passed in that run.
- No broad checks (`cargo fmt`, full clippy, full test suite) were run in this session.
- No real-agent smoke scenario was run; no Codex initialization/auth blocker was captured.
codex: reviewer findings routed back for fixes

reviewer findings:
I’ll inspect the orchestrator-provided bundle only and ground the review in the patched symbols and call paths it contains.

The patch is entirely in terminal/UI input and rendering paths, so I’m narrowing on `TerminalApp`, `TerminalUi`, `UiHarness`, and the added harness tests.

One issue class I’m checking specifically is whether the new visual cursor coordinates are bounded to the text model or only to the viewport, because that directly affects yank correctness.

I found the selection render path: `render_tui_buffer` overlays `VisualSelectionOverlay` after drawing panes, and text extraction flows through `copy_visual_selection` into `selection_text`. I’m now checking the extraction helpers against the overlay model.

The app and harness intercept `v`, `V`, `Ctrl-V`, `y`, and `Y` before generic `TerminalUi::handle_key`, so I’m checking both the real terminal adapter and the harness adapter for parity.

There’s a mismatch risk around wrapping: rendering selects terminal cells, while yank extraction works over `visible_content` line strings. I’m reading the visible-content helper and left-pane line construction to see whether that mismatch is observable.

One concrete regression is emerging: the app-level intercepts for visual/yank keys run before `TerminalUi` gets a chance to resolve pending multi-key commands such as `Ctrl-W`. That changes command-mode key sequencing, so I’m checking the exact lines for a grounded finding.

The second likely issue is about visual coordinates. `right_cursor_position_with_cursor` computes screen rows using wrapped visual rows, but `visual_start_position` stores a logical `lines().len() - 1` row. That makes right-pane visual mode start/yank from the wrong row when visible content wraps.

I also checked the stated verification evidence. The summary explicitly says only `cargo test --test ui_harness` was attempted, it failed for other feature-done tests, and no real-agent smoke or blocker was captured.

I’m doing one last pass over the left-pane model, because the left list has detail rows under agents and visual mode uses a row index derived from the selected agent rather than the rendered line index.

FINDINGS

- [P2] Pending command keys are bypassed by the new app-level `v`/`V`/`Y` intercepts. In `src/terminal_app.rs:557` and `src/terminal_app.rs:561`, command-mode `Y` and `v` run before the key reaches `TerminalUi::handle_key`; the harness duplicates this at `src/ui_harness.rs:212` and `src/ui_harness.rs:215`. That skips `TerminalUi::handle_key_inner`’s pending-key resolution at `src/ui.rs:474`, so sequences like `Ctrl-W Y` yank the focused line and leave the `Ctrl-W` pending state active instead of being treated as an unmatched pane command. The same applies to `g v`/`g Y` versus the existing `g` pending flow.

- [P2] Left-pane yanks can copy/highlight the wrong rendered row when agents have detail rows. `visual_start_position` maps left selection start to `row: self.control_selected` at `src/ui.rs:1084`, but `left_pane_text_lines` inserts extra `files`, `conflicts`, `depends-on`, and `depended-on-by` rows at `src/ui.rs:1279` and `src/ui.rs:1295`. As a result, selecting or pressing `Y` on the second/third agent after an earlier agent with modified files can copy that earlier agent’s detail line instead of the focused agent row.

- [P2] Right-pane visual selection uses logical line indexes where the renderer uses wrapped terminal rows. `visual_start_position` starts the right selection at `lines.len() - 1` and the last line’s character count at `src/ui.rs:1088`, while the actual cursor path accounts for wrapped visual rows with `visual_rows` in `right_cursor_position_with_cursor` at `src/ui.rs:1440`. `selection_text` then interprets the same row as a logical `visible_content.lines()` index at `src/ui.rs:1215`. With any wrapped transcript line or a wrapped `chat> ` prompt, `v` starts/highlights on the wrong screen row and `y` can copy the wrong text or nothing.

- [P3] Required real-agent verification is missing for this terminal chat behavior change. The provided evidence says no real-agent smoke scenario was run and no Codex initialization/auth blocker was captured. This would be resolved by a bounded run against the configured real backend that launches/selects a real agent chat and exercises the visual selection/yank path with the visible result recorded, or by the exact documented Codex pre-agent blocker plus the automated coverage used instead.
@work-leaf read src/ui.rs src/ui_harness.rs src/terminal_app.rs tests/ui_harness.rs
I’ll fix the code issues rather than restating the earlier patch. I’m reading the current orchestrator bundle to make the edits against the exact reviewed state.

The failing areas are in the current committed code: the adapters intercept visual keys before `TerminalUi` pending-key handling, and `TerminalUi` maps selection rows to control indexes/logical lines instead of rendered rows. I’m going to make the model use pane text rows for both drawing and yank text, then guard the adapter intercepts when a pending key exists.

I’m applying the fixes in three places: `TerminalUi` gets a rendered-row selection model, the adapters only intercept visual keys when no pending command key is active, and the tests cover the three reviewed regressions directly.

The patch routes selected-agent slash messages through a distinct backend command API instead of the ordinary prompt send path.

Behavior chain:
`src/workspace.rs::WorkLeafController::send_message` still appends `user: /status` to the selected session and starts the send worker. Inside `src/cli.rs::CommandChat::send_to_agent_streaming_with_ids`, `selected_agent_backend_command` detects messages whose trimmed text starts with `/` followed by a non-whitespace command token. Those messages call `AgentBackend::execute_command_streaming`; normal chat still calls `send_streaming`.

Provider surface:
`src/agent_runtime.rs::AgentBackend` adds default `execute_command` and `execute_command_streaming` methods. The defaults delegate to the existing send path so external providers still compile unless they opt into strict command handling.

Codex behavior:
`src/codex.rs::CodexBackend::build_command_invocation` requires an existing session, resolves the stored Codex thread id, and builds a raw `codex exec resume --json <thread> -` invocation with stdin exactly equal to `/status` or `/fork`. `execute_command_streaming` runs that invocation and records the command/output in the Codex session via `record_command_output`.

Coverage added:
`tests/workspace.rs::controller_executes_selected_agent_slash_commands_through_backend_command_path` covers `/status` and `/fork`, and asserts the fake backend’s ordinary `sends()` list stays empty. `tests/terminal_app.rs::terminal_app_slash_command_from_chat_view_sends_agent_command` now asserts terminal-selected `/status` reaches `commands()` rather than `sends()`. `src/codex.rs` also adds command-invocation coverage for raw `/fork`.

Verification run:
`cargo test slash_command --all-features` passed. It exercised the Codex slash unit test, terminal slash tests, UI harness slash test, and the new workspace `/status`/`/fork` command-path test.

@work-leaf done

Patch scope for `user-3` / `ce2e673a`:

The patch adds a review-completion confirmation flow for patch-agent chats. In `src/workspace.rs`, `WorkLeafController::apply_agent_result` now appends a `work-leaf: Is this feature done? yes/no` prompt to the reviewed patch agent after `CommandChatResult::ReviewComplete`. `WorkLeafController::send_message` intercepts replies while that prompt is pending: `yes`/`y` records closure without resuming the backend agent, `no`/`n` records reopening, and any other typed message clears the prompt and falls through to the normal agent send path.

The terminal side in `src/terminal_app.rs` treats that prompt as the source of chat highlight state. `TerminalAppCore::apply_session_to_ui` maps sessions whose last line is the completion prompt to `TerminalUi::set_agent_ready_state`, and `send_chat_buffer` clears the highlight when the user responds. A `yes` response hides the chat via new `TerminalUi` hidden-state helpers in `src/ui.rs`; typing another message keeps the chat visible and sends the message, which is the reopen path.

`src/ui_harness.rs` and `tests/ui_harness.rs` add harness support and coverage for the yes/no behavior: the prompt highlights the patch-agent row, `yes` closes/hides it, and ordinary typing reopens/sends. `tests/workspace.rs` adds controller coverage that review completion appends the prompt and that `yes` records `work-leaf: feature closed` without entering the backend send path.

Verification evidence:
- Attempted focused validation: `cargo test feature_done --all-targets --all-features`
- Result: blocked at compile time before tests ran.
- Exact blocker: `src/ui.rs:710` from another concurrent patch calls `frame.buffer_mut()`, but `tui 0.19` `Frame<TestBackend>` has no such method.
- Earlier broad attempt also failed on the same external compile error.
- No real-agent smoke scenario completed. This patch affects review-completion terminal chat behavior, so real-agent verification remains blocked by the same compile failure until the external `frame.buffer_mut()` issue is resolved.
codex: reviewer findings routed back for fixes

reviewer findings:
I’m going to inspect the provided context bundle first, then I’ll request any missing patch-specific context through Work Leaf rather than reading project files directly.

The bundle includes the current patched files and architecture doc. I’ll need to isolate the new review-completion behavior, especially controller state, terminal visibility/highlighting, and tests that define the intended contract.

The terminal layer marks a chat ready only when the last cached session line starts with the completion prompt, then hides it on `yes`. I’m checking the controller’s prompt bookkeeping next, because stale state there can make the UI reopen or misroute messages.

The tests cover the happy paths for `yes` and “continue working” input. I’m also checking the remote/controller event path, because this feature crosses the in-process UI, HTTP-style events, and cached terminal snapshots.

I’ve found the core flow: `WorkLeafController::apply_agent_result` creates the prompt, `send_message` consumes it, and `TerminalAppCore::apply_session_to_ui` derives the row highlight from the prompt line. I’ll check edge cases around multiple prompts and hidden/selection behavior before deciding findings.

One potential review concern is whether the patch keeps “ready” tied only to the completion prompt rather than every idle agent. The current code intentionally changed that in `apply_session_to_ui`, and there is a regression test for clearing loading without marking ready, so I won’t flag that.

No broad checks are needed for a review finding decision, but I am considering the patch agent’s validation report. They gave an exact compile-time blocker and an exact real-agent blocker tied to that compile failure, so missing real-agent verification is not automatically a finding here.

I haven’t found a behavior regression in the changed controller/UI paths. The remaining thing I’m verifying is whether the new hidden-agent helper leaves the left-pane selection in a valid state after a selected chat is closed.

FINDINGS

- `src/workspace.rs:784`: Repeating `no` for a later completion prompt can leave the prompt pending. `resolve_feature_done_prompt` records `user: no` and `work-leaf: feature reopened`, but `append_agent_line` suppresses duplicate lines across the whole session at `src/workspace.rs:738`. On a second review cycle for the same patch agent, if the user answers `no` again, both lines already exist, so nothing is appended after the new `work-leaf: Is this feature done? yes/no (...)` prompt. Then `feature_done_prompt_pending` at `src/workspace.rs:796` still sees that prompt as the last line and keeps treating future messages as prompt answers. The repeated-review reopen flow needs a non-duplicated acknowledgement or another way to mark that prompt consumed.
@work-leaf read src/workspace.rs tests/workspace.rs

I’ll fix the reviewer’s concrete stale-prompt case by making prompt consumption observable even when the same answer is repeated across review cycles, then add a controller regression test for repeated `no`.

The duplicate suppression is in `append_agent_line`, so the narrow fix is to make completion-prompt acknowledgements unique while preserving the stable prefix/text for existing assertions. I’ll add a repeated-review test that answers `no` twice, then verifies the next free-form message is sent normally.

The fix will keep the existing transcript de-duplication for normal agent output, but use a unique acknowledgement for consumed completion prompts. That directly addresses the stale-last-line condition without broadening event payloads or changing the public DTOs.

reviewer findings:
FINDINGS

- `src/workspace.rs:1051`: The O(n²) issue remains. `unique_session_line` still increments suffixes in a loop and scans `existing_lines` for each candidate, so repeated completion acknowledgements degrade quadratically. The focused test passing verifies the stale-prompt behavior, but it does not resolve the repository review rule that new O(n²) behavior must be flagged. Compute the next suffix in one pass or store prompt acknowledgement counts separately.
I’ll replace the suffix search with a single pass over the session lines. The behavior stays the same, but the helper will compute the maximum existing suffix in O(n).
The helper block in the bundle is enough to patch, but I’m checking the surrounding braces once so the replacement context is exact and doesn’t collide with the concatenated test file in the bundle.
